1: The fifth plague: YHWH's messenger-formula to Pharaoh through Moses — 'Let my people go that they may serve me.' The sixth repetition of the deliverance demand. The persistent repetition is not rhetorical failure but covenant documentation — YHWH offers the opportunity for response with every announcement. The gospel similarly does not tire of its own proclamation: 'How then will they call on him in whom they have not believed? And how are they to believe in him of whom they have never heard?' (Romans 10:14). The repeated announcement is itself the grace.
2: 'If you refuse to let them go and hold onto them' — the conditional for the fifth plague. The two verbs (me'en leshallach and machaziq bahem) describe both passive refusal and active grasping — Pharaoh both refuses to release and grips them tighter. This double resistance (refusing and gripping) describes the condition of every enslaved will: it does not merely fail to come to freedom but actively clings to bondage. Christ's deliverance addresses both the external captors and the internal resistance.
3: YHWH's hand will be on Egypt's livestock — horses, donkeys, camels, herds, flocks. The fifth plague targets the economic infrastructure of Egypt: its transportation, its agriculture, its food supply, its trade goods. The targeting is systematic — every category of working and food animal. This comprehensive economic judgment mirrors the NT's teaching that creation itself groans under sin's effects (Romans 8:20–22): every dimension of the creaturely economy is subject to the consequences of human rebellion.
4: YHWH will distinguish (hifla YHWH) between Israel's livestock and Egypt's livestock — not one of Israel's shall die. The Goshen-distinction extends to Israel's animals: the covenant protection is not only for persons but for the whole household, the whole economy of the covenant people. The NT analogue is the blessing that flows to those connected to the covenant people: 'The unbelieving husband is made holy because of his wife' (1 Corinthians 7:14). Covenant protection has a household radius.
5: YHWH set a time: 'Tomorrow YHWH will do this thing in the land.' The advance notice with a specific time-stamp is unprecedented in the plague sequence. By announcing the precise timing, YHWH increases Pharaoh's accountability while providing one more opportunity for response. The prophetic announcement of judgment with specific timing (Jonah 3:4 — 'in forty days Nineveh will be overthrown') is always also an implicit call to preemptive repentance. The deadline intensifies the mercy of the warning.
6: YHWH did that thing the next day: all Egypt's livestock died; not one of Israel's died. The contrast is starkly enacted: 'all the livestock of Egypt died' and 'not one of the livestock of Israel died' — the two outcomes sit in the same verse. The juxtaposition of total loss and total preservation is the plague narrative's most concentrated expression of the judgment/salvation binary. This binary runs from here through the Passover (death/protection) and ultimately to the cross (death for sin, life for the forgiven).
7: Pharaoh sent and found that not one of Israel's livestock had died — and his heart was hardened. The investigation confirmed the miracle; the hardening followed the confirmation. This is the most counterintuitive moment in the plague sequence: Pharaoh verifies the supernatural protection of Israel and becomes more resistant. Confirming miracles do not automatically produce faith when the heart is set against the messenger. Jesus experienced the same pattern: 'Though he had done so many signs before them, they still did not believe in him' (John 12:37).
8: YHWH tells Moses and Aaron: 'Take handfuls of soot (pigam kivshanim*) from the kiln and let Moses throw it toward the sky.' The sixth plague (boils) is generated from the kiln — the same kilns where Israel labored under Egyptian oppression (the brick-making kilns of Exodus 1:14; Exodus 5:7–19). The instrument of Israel's suffering becomes the source of Egypt's judgment. The suffering that the oppressor inflicted is now turned against the oppressor — a pattern that reaches its ultimate expression in the cross: the death that sin brought into the world becomes the means of sin's defeat.
9: The soot becomes fine dust over Egypt and causes boils on humans and animals. Boils (shchin) — painful, festering skin eruptions — afflict both people and animals. The body itself becomes the site of judgment: the skin that defines the boundary between self and world breaks down. Boils in the Hebrew law-code are one of the covenant curse conditions (Deuteronomy 28:35 — 'YHWH will strike you with the boils of Egypt'). The sixth plague is not merely a natural disaster but a covenant curse enacted on the oppressor nation.
10: Moses throws the soot upward, and boils break out on humans and animals throughout Egypt. The upward gesture is significant: the soot lifted toward heaven and falling across Egypt enacts the principle that judgment comes from above. In the NT, both blessing and judgment descend from YHWH — the same divine sovereignty that sends the rain of grace also sends the fire of judgment (Matthew 5:45; Revelation 8:5). The throwing-upward of the soot is a physical enactment of prayer-and-answer: Moses sends it up; YHWH sends it back as plague.
11: The magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils — the boils were on them too. The magicians are now afflicted by the same plague they could no longer counter: their own bodies are under the judgment. The counterfeit religious system has been rendered physically incapable of functioning — its practitioners cannot serve in the presence of the one they opposed. The final judgment will work similarly: every pretender to divine authority will be silenced by their own inability to withstand the holy presence (Revelation 6:15–17 — kings, generals, everyone hiding from 'the face of him who is seated on the throne').
12: YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart — this time the hardening is explicitly attributed to YHWH, not merely described as Pharaoh's own doing. The transition from Pharaoh hardening his own heart (Exodus 7:13, 22; Exodus 8:15, 19, 32) to YHWH hardening it (Exodus 9:12; Exodus 10:1, 20, 27) marks the theological shift Paul discusses in Romans 9:18 — at some point, the one who hardens himself is 'given over' to the hardening he chose. The divine hardening is not arbitrary but is the confirmed establishment of the choice Pharaoh has been making all along.
13: YHWH tells Moses: 'Rise early, stand before Pharaoh, and say: Let my people go that they may serve me.' The messenger-formula continues unchanged despite escalating hardness and escalating plagues. YHWH's word does not adapt to Pharaoh's resistance; the demand remains constant. The unchanging demand mirrors the gospel's unchanging content: 'Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8). What YHWH requires of Pharaoh — full surrender of Israel — is not negotiable and does not change.
14: YHWH will send all his plagues against Pharaoh's heart and servants and people — 'so that you may know there is no one like me in all the earth.' The knowledge-goal is reiterated with intensification: not just Egypt will know, but all the earth. This universal scope matches the NT's universal proclamation goal. The plagues that demonstrate YHWH's incomparability in Egypt become the precedent for the cross that demonstrates YHWH's love 'in all the earth' — 'if I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself' (John 12:32).
15: 'For by now I could have stretched out my hand and struck you and your people with pestilence and you would have been cut off from the earth.' YHWH's restraint is explicit: the plagues up to this point are not maximum judgment but measured judgment. YHWH could have destroyed Egypt entirely at any point; the prolonged plague sequence is itself an act of mercy — discipline toward potential repentance. Peter identifies the same principle in God's apparent delay of judgment: 'The Lord is not slow to fulfill his promise as some count slowness, but is patient toward you, not wishing that any should perish' (2 Peter 3:9).
16: 'But for this purpose I raised you up — to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth.' This verse is quoted by Paul in Romans 9:17 as the paradigmatic statement of divine sovereignty over history. YHWH raised up Pharaoh (he'emadticha*) — brought him to this position — not for his own benefit but as the instrument through which YHWH's power would be demonstrated and his name proclaimed. Pharaoh's resistance serves YHWH's revelation. The cross operates by the same principle: the ultimate act of human resistance to God (crucifying the Son) becomes the ultimate declaration of God's name and power to the world.
17: 'You are still exalting yourself against my people by not letting them go.' The verb mistalea (exalt yourself against) captures Pharaoh's posture: self-elevation at the expense of YHWH's people. This is the posture of all oppressive power and of the devil himself ('I will make myself like the Most High,' Isaiah 14:14). Christ's incarnation is the direct inversion of this: 'though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself' (Philippians 2:6–7). The self-exalter is humbled; the self-emptier is exalted.
18: 'Tomorrow I will cause very heavy hail to fall — such as has never been in Egypt since its founding until now.' The seventh plague is announced as unprecedented: the superlative judgment language signals that escalation is approaching its peak. The phrase 'such as has never been' will recur in the plague climax language and in eschatological texts (Daniel 12:1 — 'a time of trouble such as has never been'; Matthew 24:21 — 'great tribulation, such as has not been from the beginning of the world'). The plagues establish a literary-theological template for describing final judgment.
19: 'Now therefore send, bring your livestock and everything you have in the field to shelter — every human being and animal in the field that is not brought home will die when the hail falls on them.' The pre-plague warning contains a salvation offer: those who take cover will survive. YHWH warns before he strikes and provides a way of escape within the warning. This is the pattern of all covenant grace — judgment announced, a way of escape opened, human response determining outcome. Noah and the ark, Rahab's scarlet cord, Passover blood — all are the same pattern of provided protection within announced judgment.
20: Those among Pharaoh's servants who feared the word of YHWH (hayare et-dvar YHWH) — they brought their servants and livestock indoors. Even within Egypt, some responded to YHWH's warning with protective action. The 'fear of YHWH' within Egypt is remarkable: YHWH's words produced faith-response in some Egyptians during the plagues. The mixed response within Egypt prefigures the mixed response to the gospel within every nation — 'from every tribe and language and people and nation' (Revelation 5:9), there will be those who hear and respond.
21: Those who did not set their hearts on the word of YHWH left their servants and livestock in the field. The two responses divide Egypt along the line of the word: those who took it to heart and those who disregarded it. The word itself is the dividing line — this is what Jesus means when he says 'I came not to bring peace but a sword' (Matthew 10:34). The gospel does not divide on ethnicity or class but on response to the word. The hail plague makes visible what is true in every generation.
22: YHWH tells Moses: 'Stretch out your hand toward heaven so that hail may fall on the land of Egypt — on humans and animals and every plant of the field.' The gesture toward heaven for the hail mirrors the soot-throwing for boils (Exodus 9:10) but in the opposite direction: Moses reaches up rather than throwing up. The intercessory/mediatorial gesture connects earth to heaven in both directions — plague descends as Moses reaches toward the source of judgment. The mediator's posture determines the direction of the divine action.
23: Moses stretched out his staff toward heaven and YHWH sent thunder and hail — and fire (esh*) ran down to earth. YHWH thundered (qolot*) and fire and hail fell together. The hail-and-fire combination appears in Revelation 8:7 as the first trumpet judgment — 'hail and fire, mixed with blood, were thrown upon the earth.' The Exodus plague is the literary and theological template for Revelation's eschatological judgment imagery. The plagues of Egypt are a micro-pattern for the final plagues of history.
24: Hail and fire flashing in the midst of the hail — very heavy, such as had not been in Egypt since becoming a nation. The unprecedented severity is repeated: the superlative judgment language marks this as the most severe plague to date. The fire (lightning) within the hail reversed the normal order of nature — fire and water do not coexist. The miracle is in the combination: divine judgment operates outside natural categories. The cross similarly defies categories — the Son of God dying is not a natural event but a cosmic anomaly that splits the veil between earth and heaven (Matthew 27:51).
25: The hail struck every plant of the field and every tree in the field throughout Egypt — everything in the open, human and animal. The ecological scope: vegetation, trees, animals, people — everything in the open was struck. The reversal of the creation order that began with the Nile (water corrupted) now reaches the vegetation and animal world. The plague sequence is a systematic uncreation — reversing the good creation of Gen 1–2 domain by domain. Christ's redemption is a systematic recreation of the same domains (2 Corinthians 5:17; Romans 8:21).
26: Only in the land of Goshen, where Israel lived, there was no hail. The Goshen distinction holds through the seventh plague as through the fourth, fifth, and sixth. The cumulative Goshen exemptions build the case for covenant protection as the defining mark of YHWH's people. By the time of the Passover (Exodus 12), the pattern is fully established: what YHWH marks, the judgment passes over. The Passover blood is the explicit sign of what Goshen location expressed implicitly — the covenant covers those who belong to YHWH.
27: Pharaoh called Moses and Aaron: 'I have sinned this time. YHWH is righteous; I and my people are in the wrong.' The first explicit confession from Pharaoh — the only explicit statement of sin and of YHWH's righteousness in the entire plague narrative. The confession is real in its content but not in its depth: Pharaoh correctly identifies the categories (YHWH = righteous; we = wrong) but the confession does not penetrate to repentance. The NT distinguishes between 'worldly grief that produces death' and 'godly grief that produces repentance' (2 Corinthians 7:10). Pharaoh's confession is the former.
28: 'Pray to YHWH — there has been enough of God's thunder and hail. I will let you go; you shall stay no longer.' The prayer-petition returns — Pharaoh wants intercession but not transformation. He asks Moses to pray the plague away without committing to the covenant change the prayer would signify. This is the condition of superficial religiosity: using prayer as a technique to obtain relief without pursuing the relationship with God that genuine prayer requires.
29: Moses says: 'As soon as I have gone out of the city I will spread out my hands to YHWH — the thunder will cease and there will be no more hail, so that you may know that the earth is YHWH's.' The purpose of the prayer's answer is stated: knowledge — 'so that you may know the earth is YHWH's' (laYHWH ha'aretz*). The earth belongs to YHWH (Psalms 24:1). Every plague has been a demonstration of this ownership — the Creator reasserting his rights over his own creation against those who have claimed it for themselves (Pharaoh) or abandoned it to false gods (Egypt).
30: 'But I know that you and your servants do not yet fear YHWH God.' Moses prays and then immediately issues the prophetic diagnosis: the relief will come but the fear of YHWH has not followed the confession. Moses knows Pharaoh's heart — the pattern of relief-and-relapse has established it. The prophet sees through the surface of confession to the absence of genuine transformation beneath. Christ similarly 'knew what was in man' (John 2:25) and did not trust himself to those who believed on the basis of signs alone (John 2:23–24).
31: The flax and barley were struck — the barley was in ear and the flax was in bud at the time of the plague. The agricultural specificity grounds the plague in seasonal reality: the hail destroyed the early spring crops (barley and flax ripen in January-March in Egypt). This is not supernatural exaggeration but historically plausible timing. The specificity of divine judgment is part of its integrity — YHWH does not deal in vague catastrophe but in precisely placed intervention that is historically verifiable.
32: But the wheat and spelt were not struck — they were later crops. The partial crop destruction (early crops destroyed, late crops spared) left Egypt with diminished but not eliminated food resources. YHWH calibrated the plague to maximize warning without immediate annihilation. This calibration reflects the NT principle of structured discipline: 'He disciplines us for our good, that we may share in his holiness' (Hebrews 12:10). The goal is not destruction but the knowledge that produces change.
33: Moses left Pharaoh, went out of the city, and spread out his hands to YHWH — the thunder and hail ceased, the rain stopped pouring on the earth. The prayer was answered immediately and completely. Moses spread his hands (vayyifros kapav*) — the bodily posture of complete openness before YHWH, lifting both hands to heaven. This gesture appears throughout Scripture as the stance of intercessory prayer (1 Kings 8:22; Ezra 9:5) and ultimately finds its fullest expression in the crucifixion — arms spread wide, the ultimate intercessory posture.
34: When Pharaoh saw that the rain and hail and thunder ceased, he sinned again and hardened his heart — he and his servants. The cycle repeats with confirmation: not only Pharaoh but his servants hardened themselves. The individual hardening has become collective; the court culture has normalized resistance to YHWH's word. This community of shared hardness is the institutional form of sin — when the structures and cultures of a society are organized around the rejection of YHWH's authority, individual change becomes harder. Christ confronted the same institutional sin in the religious establishment of his day.
35: Pharaoh's heart was hardened — he did not let the Israelites go, as YHWH had spoken through Moses. The chapter closes on the same note as every previous cycle: hardness, non-release, fulfillment of YHWH's word. But the phrase 'as YHWH had spoken through Moses' (ka'asher diber YHWH beyad-Moshe) frames the refusal as confirmation rather than defeat: YHWH's word is being fulfilled even in Pharaoh's non-compliance. The intractability of the enemy does not frustrate the divine word but fulfills it — the same principle that governs the cross: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, you crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men' (Acts 2:23).
